topic: A neighbor asks to use part of the yard for a garden
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of Mrs. Henderson’s cheerful knock jolted me from my reading. I froze, the book slipping from my numb fingers. When I opened the door, she stood on her porch, beaming, clutching a trowel. "Oh, hello there! I was hoping you might be able to help me out with a little bit of space in your yard. I have a lovely idea for a vegetable patch." My throat tightened. I could feel a flush creeping up my neck, hot and undeniable. I glanced desperately toward the overgrown corner of my property, calculating how much of my prized rose bushes I could sacrifice. "Um," I managed, my voice coming out thin and reedy. "I... I don't know. I'm not sure I can."

[story 2]

I was wrestling with a stubborn knot in my old fishing net when the doorbell rang. It was Mr. Davies, my neighbor, looking unusually earnest. "Excuse me," he began, his voice pitched just a little too loudly, "I was wondering if you might consider letting me use a section of your lawn. I'm planning a very ambitious herb garden." My hands immediately stilled on the netting. A strange, prickly heat bloomed behind my eyes. I tried to focus on the perfectly manicured hedge, but my gaze kept darting toward the patch of grass he was implicitly pointing at. "Oh," I mumbled, pulling my shoulders inward as if trying to shrink. "Well, I suppose I could look at it." The silence stretched, heavy and awkward, punctuated only by the buzzing of a nearby bee.

[story 3]

It happened while I was watering my succulents. A shadow fell across my patio, and I looked up to see Sarah from down the street standing there, holding a gardening brochure. "Hi!" she chirped, her enthusiasm radiating outward. "I have this wonderful idea for a small flower bed, and I was hoping—would you mind if I borrowed a bit of your yard for a while?" My breath hitched. My palms suddenly felt clammy, slick with nervous sweat. I gripped the watering can so tightly my knuckles turned white. I wanted to bolt, to pretend I had suddenly developed an urgent need to check the plumbing. "Oh," I managed, my voice sounding far too high-pitched. "I... I haven't really thought about it."
